But even if they are all distinct, they might still be colinear. So the main check is whether the three points lie on a straight line. How do I check that?

The standard way to check if three points are colinear is to calculate the area of the triangle they form. If the area is zero, they are colinear. The area can be calculated using the determinant method. The formula for the area given three points (x1,y1), (x2,y2), (x3,y3) is:

Area = 0.5 * | (x1*(y2 - y3) + x2*(y3 - y1) + x3*(y1 - y2)) |

But since we only care if the area is zero, we can ignore the 0.5 and the absolute value. So the determinant part should be zero if they are colinear. public class Solution {
    public boolean isBoomerang(int[][] points) {
        // Check duplicates
        if ( (points[0][0] == points[1][0] && points[0][1] == points[1][1]) ||
             (points[0][0] == points[2][0] && points[0][1] == points[2][1]) ||
             (points[1][0] == points[2][0] && points[1][1] == points[2][1]) ) {
            return false;
        }

        int x1 = points[0][0], y1 = points[0][1];
        int x2 = points[1][0], y2 = points[1][1];
        int x3 = points[2][0], y3 = points[2][1]

        int det = x1*(y2 - y3) + x2*(y3 - y1) + x3*(y1 - y2);

        return det !=0;
    }
}

solution: public boolean isBoomerang(int[][] points) {
    if ((points[0][0] == points[1][0] && points[0][1] == points[1][1]) ||
        (points[0][0] == points[2][0] && points[0][1] == points[2][1]) ||
        (points[1][0] == points[2][0] && points[1][1] == points[2][1])) {
        return false;
    }
    int x1 = points[0][0], y1 = points[0][1];
    int x2 = points[1][0], y2 = points[1][1];
    int x3 = points[2][0], y3 = points[2][1];
    return (x1*(y2 - y3) + x2*(y3 - y1) + x3*(y1 - y2)) != 0;
}
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(1)
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution first checks for duplicate points, ensuring all are distinct. It then uses a determinant calculation to check collinearity, avoiding floating-point operations for precision. Constant time and space operations ensure optimal performance.
